Up to 185 [MB](/wiki/Megabytes "Megabytes") + 5 min analog LaserDisc video

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22CD+Video%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22CD+Video%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22CD+Video%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22CD+Video%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(December 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(also known as **CDV**, **CD-V**, or **CD+V**) was a format of [optical media disc](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") that was introduced in 1987 that combines the technologies of standard [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") and [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc"). CD-V discs are the same size as a standard 12 cm (4.7 in) audio CD, and contain up to 20 minutes' worth of [CD audio](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio "Compact Disc Digital Audio") that can be played on any [audio CD player](/wiki/CD_player "CD player"). They also contain up to 5 minutes of analog LaserDisc video information with a digital CD-quality soundtrack, which can be played back on a newer [LaserDisc player](/wiki/LaserDisc_player "LaserDisc player") capable of playing CD-V discs or CD-V-only players.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This article is about the short-lived LaserDisc/CD hybrid from the late 1980s; not to be confused with the later, better-known format, [Video CD](/wiki/Video_CD "Video CD").

Hybrid analog video/digital audio format

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/CD_Video "Special:EditPage/CD Video") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["CD Video"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22CD+Video%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22CD+Video%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22CD+Video%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22CD+Video%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22CD+Video%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22CD+Video%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(December 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

CD Video (CDV)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
|  | |
| Media type | [Optical disc](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") |
| [Encoding](/wiki/Content_format "Content format") | analog video + digital audio |
| Capacity | Up to 185 [MB](/wiki/Megabytes "Megabytes") + 5 min analog LaserDisc video |
| Read mechanism | 780 nm wavelength semiconductor laser |
| [Standard](/wiki/International_standard "International standard") | IEC 61104（12cm） |
| Developed by | [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips"), [Panasonic](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic"), [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") |
| Usage | audio and video storage |
| Released | 1987 |

**CD Video** (also known as **CDV**, **CD-V**, or **CD+V**) was a format of [optical media disc](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") that was introduced in 1987 that combines the technologies of standard [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") and [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc"). CD-V discs are the same size as a standard 12 cm (4.7 in) audio CD, and contain up to 20 minutes' worth of [CD audio](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio "Compact Disc Digital Audio") that can be played on any [audio CD player](/wiki/CD_player "CD player"). They also contain up to 5 minutes of analog LaserDisc video information with a digital CD-quality soundtrack, which can be played back on a newer [LaserDisc player](/wiki/LaserDisc_player "LaserDisc player") capable of playing CD-V discs or CD-V-only players.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The "CD Video" brand was also used to market some 20 cm (7.9 in) and 30 cm (12 in) LaserDiscs which included a digital soundtrack[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2) but no CD-compatible content.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Video&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/CD-Video-all-sizes-1.jpg/250px-CD-Video-all-sizes-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:CD-Video-all-sizes-1.jpg)

12 cm, 20 cm and 30 cm "CD-Video" discs. Despite the name, only the smallest contains CD-compatible content. The larger two are effectively Laserdiscs with a digital soundtrack.

### 12 cm "CD Video" disc format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Video&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 12 cm \"CD Video\" disc format")]

One of the first LaserDisc players that can play CD-V discs is the [Pioneer CLD-1010](/wiki/Pioneer_CLD-1010 "Pioneer CLD-1010") from 1987. Though it is a CD-based format, CD Video was never given a [rainbow book](/wiki/Rainbow_Books "Rainbow Books") designation; the idea of encoding [analog video](/wiki/Analog_video "Analog video"), which is incompatible between different regions, was poorly received by CD stakeholders other than Philips, who had not consulted them prior to demonstrating the format to the music industry.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

CD Video discs have a distinctive gold color,[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2)[[4]](#cite_note-promovid-4) to differentiate them from regular silver-colored audio CDs. This is a characteristic that would later be replicated in [HVD](/wiki/Holographic_versatile_disc "Holographic versatile disc"), a more advanced disc format.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/CDV2.jpg/250px-CDV2.jpg)](/wiki/File:CDV2.jpg)

A typical 12 cm CD Video release in its case. This contains three audio tracks and one video by the band [Lloyd Cole and the Commotions](/wiki/Lloyd_Cole_and_the_Commotions "Lloyd Cole and the Commotions").

The physical size of the 12 cm (4.7 in) discs limited the amount of LaserDisc content[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2) to around six minutes, which meant they were primarily suited to [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") [music videos](/wiki/Music_video "Music video").[[5]](#cite_note-newsci_88_05_12-5) However, both players and discs were too expensive for the youth market likely to be most interested in such content.[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2)

### Other uses of the name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Video&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other uses of the name")]

#### "CD Video" branding on 20 and 30 cm LaserDiscs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Video&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: \"CD Video\" branding on 20 and 30 cm LaserDiscs")]

The "CD Video" name and logo were also used to market some full-size 30 cm (12 in) and EP-sized 20 cm (7.9 in) LaserDiscs that featured a digital (rather than analog) soundtrack and the same gold colouring.[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2)

Unlike the newly launched 12 cm (4.7 in) discs, these were little more than a rebrand of existing LaserDisc formats.[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2) (Both disc sizes, along with support for digital soundtracks, had been in use for several years before CD Video launched.)[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2) In addition, despite the name, they did not feature CD-compatible content as the smaller discs did.

#### European LaserDisc relaunch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Video&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: European LaserDisc relaunch")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/CD_Video_on_top_of_case_showing_basic_design_i.jpg/250px-CD_Video_on_top_of_case_showing_basic_design_i.jpg)](/wiki/File:CD_Video_on_top_of_case_showing_basic_design_i.jpg)

CD Video showing refraction patterns which vary between the regions holding CD audio (inside) and LaserDisc (outside)

Philips' launch of the CD Video format (and the rebranding of existing LaserDisc formats under the name) also served as the basis of a relaunch for LaserDisc as a whole in Europe[[6]](#cite_note-newscientist_1552-6) where sales under the original "LaserVision" name had been extremely poor,[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2)[[6]](#cite_note-newscientist_1552-6) and Philips attempted to leverage the name recognition of the newly successful [audio CD](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio "Compact Disc Digital Audio") format.[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2)[[4]](#cite_note-promovid-4) Despite this, the rebranded format remained unsuccessful in Europe.[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2)

The use of digital audio on the relaunched "CD Video"-branded LaserDiscs rendered them incompatible with older analog-only "LaserVision" players made for the European [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") television standard.[[6]](#cite_note-newscientist_1552-6) (PAL LaserDiscs can only support analog or uncompressed digital soundtracks, but not both.)[[7]](#cite_note-blam1_231-7)[[8]](#cite_note-blam1_232-8) However, at that point, Philips estimated there to have been just 12,000 to 15,000 analog-only Laservision players sold in Europe.[[6]](#cite_note-newscientist_1552-6)

### Discontinuation and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Video&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Discontinuation and legacy")]

CD Video lasted only a few years in the marketplace and was already being referred to as a "failed" format by mid-1990.[[9]](#cite_note-tu_vsd-9)

A similar format called [Video Single Disc](/wiki/Video_Single_Disc "Video Single Disc") (VSD) was later announced for the Japanese market in 1990.[[9]](#cite_note-tu_vsd-9) While this used the same CD-sized 12 cm format as CD Video, it contained only an analog video track and no CD-compatible audio[[9]](#cite_note-tu_vsd-9) making it, in effect, a small LaserDisc. The price of VSDs was intended to be around half that of CD Video discs.[[9]](#cite_note-tu_vsd-9)

When CD Video failed to become the success Philips was hoping for, they turned their attention to the more promising [MPEG-1](/wiki/MPEG-1 "MPEG-1")-based digital video compression which ultimately formed the basis of [Video CD](/wiki/Video_CD "Video CD")[[2]](#cite_note-techmoan-2)—a similarly named, but incompatible and fundamentally different (and all-digital) format launched in 1993.

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Video&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: References")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** ["Digital audio modulation in the PAL and NTSC video disc formats, J. Audio Eng. Soc. vol. 32, pp. 883, 1984"](http://www.aes.org/e-lib/browse.cfm?elib=11765). October 1983. Retrieved 2008-03-04.
2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-7) [***i***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-8) [***j***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-9) [***k***](#cite_ref-techmoan_2-10) 
   "[Techmoan](/wiki/Techmoan "Techmoan")" (Matthew "Mat" Taylor) (2018-02-01). [*CD Video (Not Video CD) - when Videodiscs went gold*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6u2j1Q8uCgQ). Retrieved 2021-05-08. "  
   *[08:27]* in Europe [Philips' LaserDisc sales were pitiful but] with Laserdisc now capable of [digital audio] why not just [restart and rebrand as] CD Video [and] piggyback on the success of compact disc  
   *[00:10]* CD Video came [on] gold colored discs  
   *[11:44]* [30 cm discs] are just Laserdiscs coloured gold and with [digital soundtrack]  
   *[13:38]* Most [discs were] smaller ones with pop music [whose market] tends to be younger people [for whom player and discs were] too expensive.  
   *[14:31]* Players for Digital Audio soundtrack Laserdiscs had existed since 1984 [..] [20 cm discs had also] been out for a number of years.  
   *[15:12]* [12 cm disc is] essence of the format [..] [Larger discs were] caught up in the rebranding but really those are Laserdiscs  
   *[15:57]* physical limitations of [12 cm disc dictated] end product because you've only got room for about six minutes worth of video  
   *[17:18]* Philips [..] started to concentrate their attentions on [more promising MPEG-1 based] digital video [ultimately resulting in] Video CD that Philips released in the early 1990s  
   *[17:45]* [Philips] tried to sell Laservision since the late 1970s without any success this CD Video rebranding hadn't [helped], and within just a couple of years the CD Video name disappeared"
3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** ["Problems ahead for CD Video"](https://web.archive.org/web/20250320174303/https://books.google.com/books/content?id=S0vI7Yz2550C&pg=PA30&img=1&zoom=3&hl=en&sig=ACfU3U0LUzFPfjPXE9RM7bT-rbZDYitltA&w=1280). *[New Scientist](/wiki/New_Scientist "New Scientist")*. 1986-12-11. Archived from [the original](https://books.google.com/books?id=S0vI7Yz2550C&q=%22problems+ahead+for+CD+video%22&pg=PA30) on 2025-03-20. Retrieved 2018-07-31.
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-promovid_4-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-promovid_4-1) 
   [*CD Video PROMO 1988 - Presented by Kenny Everett*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HOAeEf4D0dU). Philips. 1988. [Archived](https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211213/HOAeEf4D0dU) from the original on 2021-12-13. Retrieved 2021-05-08. "  
   *[0:57]* This is it- a CD machine that also plays pictures [..] and you already know how fabulous [CDs] are [..] Gone are the days of naff and fuzzy records [..]  
   *[1:21]* But now CDs come in gold too"
5. **[^](#cite_ref-newsci_88_05_12_5-0)** Barry Fox (1988-05-12). ["Roll over Berliner"](https://web.archive.org/web/20250920133735/https://books.google.com/books/content?id=nfJPdScNqJsC&pg=PA48&img=1&zoom=3&hl=en&sig=ACfU3U2kLhLZ6lqfQdK0uXcoluHmMCg_rg&w=1280). *[New Scientist](/wiki/New_Scientist "New Scientist")*. p. 48. Archived from [the original](https://books.google.com/books?id=nfJPdScNqJsC&q=%22cd+video%22&pg=PA48) on 2025-09-20. Retrieved 2021-05-09. "[a] system called CD Video (CDV) [which] will store six minutes of video - an ideal length for pop music"
6. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-newscientist_1552_6-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-newscientist_1552_6-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-newscientist_1552_6-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-newscientist_1552_6-3) Barry Fox (1987-03-19). ["Compact disc video hits the streets..."](https://web.archive.org/web/20250917112323/https://books.google.com/books/content?id=qxwPsfm2RS8C&pg=PA28&img=1&zoom=3&hl=en&sig=ACfU3U3jJPMsDFV0q3CACovUyb-l5P__0Q&w=1280) *[New Scientist](/wiki/New_Scientist "New Scientist")*. No. 1552. p. 28. Archived from [the original](https://books.google.com/books?id=qxwPsfm2RS8C&q=philips) on 17 September 2025. "Philips also sees CD Video as a way of relaunching the [..] commercially unsuccessful Laservision [whose name] will be dropped and the new name and logo used [for] 12, 20 and 30 centimentre [discs] [..] The old LaserVision videodiscs had analogue sound but new CDV discs will have digital sound [..] this makes all new discs incompatible with old [analogue-only] players in Europe. [..] Philips estimates [between] 12,000 and 15,000 old-format Laservision players in Europe"
7. **[^](#cite_ref-blam1_231_7-0)** ["The LaserDisc FAQ: 2.3.1 Analog channels"](http://www.blam1.com/LaserDisc/FAQ/FAQ_intro.htm#AdvantagesAudioAnalog). "This can't be done with PAL discs, where analog and uncompressed digital channels are mutually exclusive."
8. **[^](#cite_ref-blam1_232_8-0)** ["The LaserDisc FAQ: 2.3.2 Uncompressed digital channels, or PCM channels"](http://www.blam1.com/LaserDisc/FAQ/FAQ_intro.htm#AdvantagesAudioDigital). "PAL LDs can only have either analog or uncompressed digital audio."
9. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-tu_vsd_9-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-tu_vsd_9-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-tu_vsd_9-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-tu_vsd_9-3) ["More on Laserdiscs"](https://web.archive.org/web/20240427124538/https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=cZfZ65Q53msC&dq=%22video+single+disc%22&pg=PA3&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q=%22video%20single%20disc%22&f=false). *Video Week via Telecommunications Update*. TV & Film Service, USIA, USIS. 1990-08-13. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780316937610](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780316937610 "Special:BookSources/9780316937610"). Archived from [the original](https://books.google.com/books?id=cZfZ65Q53msC&dq=%22video+single+disc%22&pg=PA3) on 2024-04-27. "A new laserdisc format- video single disc (VSD)- is due in Japan. The 4.75 inch disc is the size of a standard CD, but carries five minutes of video and audio so it can be played in a multidisc player. It differs from the failed CD-Video in the amount of audio (the latter had 20 minutes of audio and five minutes of video) and in price (in Japan it will cost half as much as CDV or about $8 list vs. $16)"
